speaker: patient
text: Hi, Doctor. I’ve been feeling really off lately—tired all the time and a bit lightheaded when I stand up.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for coming in. Let’s start with your vitals and review your medications. When did these symptoms begin?

speaker: patient
text: About two weeks ago—gradually got worse, especially after my morning insulin dose.

speaker: doctor
text: Your systolic blood pressure today is 178 mmHg—elevated, especially given your history of hypertension.

speaker: patient
text: Yeah, I’ve been checking at home—it’s been hovering around 175–180 most mornings.

speaker: doctor
text: Your fasting blood glucose this morning was 9.5 mmol/L—higher than your target range.

speaker: doctor
text: You’re currently on Insulin glargine 10 units nightly. Has your dose felt adequate? Any episodes of hypoglycemia?

speaker: doctor
text: Your heart rate is 75 bpm—within normal limits—and your rhythm is regular.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, fatigue is the main thing—I’m sleeping enough but wake up exhausted. Also some mild dizziness.

speaker: doctor
text: Let’s check your feet for neuropathy and listen to your heart and lungs. Any chest pain or shortness of breath?

speaker: patient
text: No chest pain, but I’ve noticed my ankles feel a little puffy in the evenings.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your elevated BP and new edema, I’m going to add Lisinopril 20 mg once daily—start tomorrow morning.

speaker: patient
text: Okay, I’ll do that. Should I still take the evening insulin as usual?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes—and your glucose just rechecked at 5.9 mmol/L, which is much better—likely reflecting improved control since your last dose.

speaker: patient
text: That’s interesting—I didn’t eat anything between readings, so maybe the insulin kicked in later than usual?

speaker: doctor
text: Possibly. We’ll monitor closely. Also, your BP has dropped a bit since we started Lisinopril—let me retake it.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been taking my home BP cuff more often—I think it’s trending down, but not consistently.

speaker: doctor
text: This reading shows systolic at 149 mmHg—definitely improved from the 178 earlier.

speaker: doctor
text: And your heart rate is now 59 bpm—slightly bradycardic; let’s hold off on further antihypertensive escalation for now.

speaker: patient
text: Wait—I just checked my wrist monitor again: it says 75 bpm and 178 systolic, same as that first reading you took. Maybe the cuff was loose before?
